source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 10.2.2 Extracting a tar backup (pp. 183-185)

questions.q: Extract ALL files from the uncompressed backup /backups/etc.133.tar into the current directory.
questions.why: The x key extracts; xvf works on both compressed and uncompressed archives.

questions.q: What does the tar x key do?
options: Extracts files from a backup (compressed or not)
questions.why: x extracts; c creates, t lists.

questions.q: Extract all files from the bzip2-compressed backup /backups/home.133.tar.bz2 into the current directory.
questions.why: xvfj extracts a bzip2 archive; since tar auto-detects compression, plain xvf also works for listing/extracting.

questions.q: Extract ONLY the file etc/exports from the backup /backups/etc.133.tar.
questions.why: Naming a path after the archive extracts just that file (restored under its path in the current directory).

questions.q: Extract all files with the extension .conf from backup.tar using a wildcard.
questions.why: --wildcards lets a shell-style pattern select the files to extract (quote the pattern so the shell doesn't expand it).

questions.q: To restore files to their ORIGINAL directory with 'tar xvfP', what must be true of how the backup was made?
options: It must have been created with the P key (absolute path)
It must have been created compressed
It must have been created with the r key
questions.why: Absolute restore (xvfP) only works if the backup itself was made in absolute mode (cvfP); take care extracting absolute backups.

questions.q: What should you always do before extracting a backup, especially an absolute one?
options: Check/view the backup's contents first (tar tvf)
questions.why: Always list the contents before restoring; absolute-mode backups can overwrite files in their original locations.
